file_verdicts.summary: Standard AppImage PKGBUILD with pinned checksum from official upstream.

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/dida-appimage.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-9_45br9v...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-9_45br9v
Analyzing dida-appimage AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-9_45br9v) with anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is straightforward and follows standard AUR packaging practices. The `source` array downloads a single AppImage binary from `cdn.dida365.cn`, which is the official CDN for the Dida365 application (the upstream vendor). A SHA256 checksum is provided and pinned to a specific value, which is good practice for binary downloads.

There is no top-level command substitution, no `pkgver()` function, and no code that executes during PKGBUILD sourcing beyond simple variable assignments. The `noextract` array correctly prevents automatic extraction of the AppImage. All potentially dangerous operations (chmod, AppImage extraction, sed modifications, file installations) are confined to `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions, which do not run under `--nobuild --noprepare`.
</details>
<summary>Standard AppImage packaging from official upstream CDN; no top-level execution risks.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard AppImage packaging from official upstream CDN; no top-level execution risks.[/]

! Reviewed dida-8.0.10-x86_64.AppImage. Status: SKIPPED -- Skipping binary file: dida-8.0.10-x86_64.AppImage
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for an AppImage package. It downloads the Dida (滴答清单) AppImage from the official CDN (`cdn.dida365.cn`), which is the upstream vendor's own distribution channel. The download is protected by a pinned SHA256 checksum, providing integrity verification.

The `prepare()` function extracts the AppImage using `--appimage-extract` (standard AppImage tooling), `build()` makes routine `.desktop` file adjustments, and `package()` installs files into `$pkgdir` following conventional AppImage packaging patterns. There are no suspicious network requests, no obfuscated code, no use of `eval`, no data exfiltration, and no execution of externally fetched code beyond the declared upstream source.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AppImage PKGBUILD with pinned checksum from official upstream.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/2] Reviewing ...

Audit Skips:

/home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-9_45br9v/dida-8.0.10-x86_64.AppImage: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: dida-8.0.10-x86_64.AppImage

data.reasoning: Both models performed an initial PKGBUILD-only audit and judged the top-level scope SAFE, but neither could complete a full audit because `makepkg --nobuild` failed to download the source AppImage. Zero files beyond the PKGBUILD's top-level scope were actually reviewed. The PKGBUILD itself appears to follow a standard AppImage packaging pattern: a single source from the official Dida365 CDN (cdn.dida365.cn) with a pinned sha256sum, and all chmod/extraction/sed/install operations confined to prepare/build/package functions. However, because the prepare(), build(), and package() function bodies were not analyzed in detail (and the .install file, if any, was not reviewed), the full safety cannot be confirmed. The likely verdict is safe given the simple pattern, but it remains technically inconclusive due to incomplete coverage.
data.disagreement_analysis: No substantive disagreement — both models reached identical conclusions (SAFE on top-level PKGBUILD scope, then SKIPPED due to source download failure). The trigger for this review was the shallow coverage (0 files reviewed), not a verdict conflict.
coverage_issues: Neither model reviewed prepare()/build()/package() function bodies in depth — only the top-level scope was assessed before the gate.
Source download failed (makepkg --nobuild), so the AppImage and any extracted content / install scripts were never fetched or examined.
No .install hook file was confirmed present or reviewed.
The full PKGBUILD function logic (chmod +x, --appimage-extract, sed edits, file installs) was acknowledged but not actually audited line-by-line.
false_negatives: The actual contents of prepare()/build()/package() were not scrutinized — a malicious sed/chmod or a sneaky network call inside these functions could be missed (though initial review suggests standard patterns).
Possible .install scriptlet (post_install/post_upgrade) not verified — these execute with root privileges and are a common attack vector for AppImage packages.
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD prepare(), build(), and package() function bodies
Verify source URL host (cdn.dida365.cn) and that sha256sum is a real pinned hash, not SKIP
Desktop file and icon handling / extraction logic from the AppImage
learnings: Source download failure should not reduce an audit to near-zero coverage; the tool should still statically review all PKGBUILD function bodies and .install scripts even when the binary source cannot be fetched, since these are present in the cloned git repo independent of the download.

The 'initial PKGBUILD gate' (top-level scope only) is too narrow to constitute a meaningful audit — when makepkg fails, fall back to a full static review of the local repo files rather than skipping entirely.
Distinguish 'cannot verify downloaded binary contents' (genuinely inconclusive) from 'cannot review local PKGBUILD/install scripts' (always possible). The skip reason here conflated both.
Add a retry / mirror fallback or allow SKIP-checksum static fetch when CDN download fails, to improve completion rate for AppImage packages.
For AppImage packages specifically, the audit should always flag and review the extraction and install-time logic since the binary payload is opaque.
